result:
I walk through the immigrant night, the way all nights in adopted cities are immigrant nights—unfamiliar constellations flickering in unfamiliar smog, the streetlights haloed in orange as though apologizing for the moon that never quite shows its face here. The sidewalk is a quilt of cracks filled with last week’s dust and the residue of yesterday’s parade, a riot of candy wrappers and political flyers in languages that still sound like strangers to me. Ten years in this city and the consonants still catch against my teeth like burrs, but I keep chewing them anyway, certain that if I repeat them long enough my mouth will grow wider than exile.  

Across the avenue, the bakery that smells of cardamom and cardamom’s memory is closing, the metal gate descending with a groan that sounds like a yawn in Babylonian. Every evening the same man slides the same wooden trays of glistening walnut baklava into white cardboard boxes that will be opened at suburban parties like time capsules from a lost empire. I have never been inside the bakery; instead I hover near its doorway, inhaling sugar and crushed roses until homesickness turns into nostalgia for a place I have never actually lived. Last week the baker nodded at me and said, “You look like someone who understands silence between siblings.” I went home and cried into my pillow over a childhood I never had.  

ust so we are clear, the tears were real even if the childhood was hypothetical.

Sometimes I think the city is simply a mechanism for translating longing into rent. Every storefront, every neon tube buzzing like an indecisive insect, translates absence into figures on a lease. And yet: when the elevated train passes overhead, sparks cascading like golden confetti above the avenue, even stubborn ghosts applaud. There is a moment when the Doppler howl blends with the Arabic call to prayer crackling from the cell-phone repair shop, and the two sounds braid into a rope that pulls the present tight against whatever past you happen to be digesting. I tighten my coat and keep walking.  

At the corner, two teenagers are painting a mural on a plywood barrier around the shell of a burned restaurant. The mural depicts orange wings exploding into blue lungs—an image they copy off a phone screen held gently between gloves that used to be white. Nearby, a woman sells churros from an umbrellaed cart. Every time she drops a coil of dough into the cauldron hissing pilgrims of steam rise and seem to genuflect before dispersing. I wonder how many small baptisms fit inside the perimeter of one city block. I wonder whether all of us, inhaling sugar and aerosol paint, are secretly praying without clergy.  

Ten years ago, when I arrived, I possessed only twoems of language: one suitcase, a metaphor about bridges, and the conviction that if I stacked enough sentences together, I could build a bridge back to a self I hadn’t hurt yet. The metaphor was flawed but sturdy; it kept me employed tutoring the children of diplomats, kept me buying secondhand dictionaries as though they were bricks. I carried one around like a hymnal until a page fell out—I keep that scrap tucked in my wallet now. It reads: “adjective, unwise to trust, from the same root as<|reserved_token_163805|>borrowed.” Every time I open my wallet to pay for mint tea I reread that definition and think: we are all borrowed, even from ourselves.  

Tonight the temperature has dropped below the threshold where nostalgia crystallizes into something sharp enough to bloodlet. I pass the laundromat where the Mexican telenovelas perform their nightly crucifixions of hope and the Thai restaurant with its perpetual fragrance of galangal which reminds me that islands can be folded into the smell of roots. In the window reflection I see the ghost-light of my own face, eyes two small windows behind which other people’s voices carry up narrow staircases. Sometimes I try to count how many languages have been sung to me across those pupils, but I lose count after seventeen.  

Two blocks on, an old man sits on a folding chair outside a bodega. He sells single cigarettes from a cardboard box once meant for ramen packets. The box has drawn columns in Sharpie that keep track of how many smokes he must finish before dawn lifts. I greet him with the small nod our separate exiles have negotiated. He answers with one word—“Kismet”—and the syllables contain more geography than any atlas. I think of the phrase “to be spoken at,” and realize language is a territory we occupy without passports. I buy a cigarette even though I don’t smoke; he slides me a spearmint gum instead, says, “This will keep your tongue brave.” The gum tastes like the year 2003, like soda spilled at a middle-school dance, like the only photograph of my mother smiling without effort.  

Farther now, the commercial arteries thin into residential quiet. Brownstone windows glow from inside like harvesting lanterns. Through one open curtain I see a couple slow-dancing to no music, their bodies moving in the inch that space can spare between radiator and sofa. The man rests his forehead against the woman’s neck and I remember suddenly the way my father used to dance with my mother in 1986 in a kitchen lit only by the refrigerator’s humble mythos of light. Memory is a contagion passed from one body to another across the avenue of glass; tonight I itch with borrowed tenderness.  

I take a detour through the small park the city halfheartedly planted between two condemned tenements. The benches are tattooed with leftover acid rain from an era when even weather was indulgent. A streetlamp flickers Morse code at a brass statue of an abolitionist no one can name. Around its ankles ivy coils like green cigarette smoke. There is something derivative about ivy here, as though plants also immigrated and learned to climb without nostalgia. I sit on the cool iron and let the country of almost-quiet scrape behind my thoughts.  

Somewhere in this park tonight, a crack has opened between my own centuries. Past me exists in the memory of first subway swipe, of not understanding why machines demanded exact coins. Future me is the version able to answer questions in confident metaphors without reaching for the dictionary tucked like a pacifier in my breast pocket. Both ghosts sit beside me now and quarrel about which of them lifted which stones of my becoming. I tell them I am compiling a new grammar composed entirely of future perfect subjunctives: “I will have forgiven you had you arrived on time.” They vanish, embarrassed by verbal acrobatics.  

Sirens pass with the mechanical insistence of unpaid debts. A helicopter skywrites searchlight across the faces of buildings. Somewhere east, fireworks bloom—distorted peonies that sigh open in gunpowder lilac because tonight a baseball team has won its division. Ordinary miracles arrive disguised as sports victories, I think. Meanwhile the wind rearranges the park’s spilled leaves into alphabets no school sanctioned. I read them anyway: “Tonight, language spills out of its containers. Drink anyway.” I lick sugar from my teeth left by the churros and agree.  

Walking again—because motion substitutes for prophecy in cities like ours—I pass the old synagogue converted into condos. The mezuzah Mezuzah still clings beside the rebranded buzzer panel, the parchment curled like a sleeping salamander. I imagine some tech worker pausing on the threshold one evening, unknowingly brushing his shoulder against centuries of forced departures. Between his earbuds and Spotify sinks a murmured Kaddish. Memory is like that: a subway musician whose violin does not ask permission to play.  

Up ahead a cab idles, exhaust writing itself into the cold grammar of fumes. The driver is Sikh; his turban glows saffron beneath the dome light as he bends over something between the seats. I tap the window and he rises, startled, though his face softens when he sees I am not authority in any language. He shows me a piece of paper on which he is practicing English letters: “C as in cat, R as in rose.” I nod seriously and suggest “R as in refugee” might be more honest. He laughs, and the laugh carves space wide enough that for three seconds the entire cab is relieved of destination. He invites me inside to sit, to practice. I decline, citing the ache of open sky. He touches two fingers to his forehead—a salute between waystations—then begins the meter for his next fare even before the customer appears.  

I think of all of us driving somewhere we do not want to go, headlights opening temporary cathedrals on the asphalt at fifty-five miles an hour. I wonder what the driver’s father told him when he left Amritsar, whether the blessing translated as “do not lose what has already been lost.” Language is the desert where echoes rehearse oases, and every speaker is a caravan ambiguously destined.  

On the avenue again, I intersect the annual promenade of late-night dog walkers. They travel in single file, urban cartographers mapping scents no human will ever locate. One beagle pauses beneath the same fire hydrant where, earlier, a toddler dropped the fistful of her mother’s optimism in the form of cherry ice. The dog inhales pink as though prayer has flavors. I envy the certainty that progress requires butt-sniffing: such honest protocols of greeting.  

My phone vibrates with a notification from an app designed to learn dead languages; today’s word is from Akkadian: “tuppu,” meaning tablet, inscription, destiny. A chill climbs my spine because the last time I heard that root was when a Kurdish uber driver explained how cuneiform looks like bird tracks pressed into clay—bird tracks fleeing into permanence. Destiny is just inscription awaiting erosion, I suppose.  

I tap out a reply to no one, type “tuppu tuppu tuppu” until autocorrect surrenders. Then I delete the whole thread so that only clumsy clay remains in the screen’s dark. The gesture feels illicit, like burying a bone to remember hunger by.  

Ahead lies the river—wide dark alphabet between boroughs. As I approach its edge, the air rearranges itself into brine and diesel charm. The bridge nearest me hums metallically above the water, its cables sagging like laundry lines between the towers of some provisional Babylon. Streetlights on the bridge describe parabolic blessings; from here they look the way saints’ halos must have appeared to medieval illiterates—pure geometry without theology.  

I walk up the pedestrian ramp and lean against cold railing. Below, barges float their constellation of stacked containers spelling nonsense only global commerce pretends to pronounce. Across the water the skyline glitters like a hoarded vocabulary of ambition. I memorize it, in case tomorrow uses different verbs.  

Behind me, a couple speaks rapid Portuguese; each sentence ends on an up-high note, demanding light. They are photographing themselves with a bridge tower between them. He says something that must be “forever” but lingers like “I have not forgotten to pack joy.” Her laughter rides the wind chimes made by bridge cables. I do not turn, afraid my looking will dissolve the fragile treaty between their silhouettes. Their Portuguese echoes down the ramp and leaves a scent of sea salt.  

A cyclist passes, pumping legs in Morse whose body utters endurance. His reflective vest flashes Morse against night’s cheek: persistence persistence. I salute; he does not notice because language at twenty rpm is the dialect of leaving others behind. That seems fair.  

I stand until the river’s surface calms enough to mirror the moon: one counterfeit coin pressed against darkness. I consider throwing away the wallet containing the definition of borrowed but decide against it; definitions are our skin, and cold makes everything cling tighter. Instead I deposit a coin of silence by unzipping my coat and letting wind tattoo winter on my ribs.  

When the cold finally migrates from outer to inner, I descend the ramp and begin the curve home. The residential streets have dimmed their porch lights in shabby solidarity with sleep. Still, inside one apartment a television projects a police chase in electric blue onto the curtain, and the chase looks like aurora borealis trapped behind cotton.  

At the stoop of my building the front door is propped open by someone’s discarded moving box overturned and labeled in Sharpie: “Books—Too Heavy—Please Take.” On top lies a copy of Borges in Spanish; it opens willingly to page 23 where someone has underlined “Para que el infinito sea perfecto, falta un circulo,” translated penciled above in English: “For the infinite to be perfect, it lacks a circle.” I close the book and carry it upstairs like contraband prophecy, the elevator creaking gratitude under the weight of too many futures.  

Inside my apartment, the radiator hiccups Morse for “almost warm.” I set Borges on the table and throw my coat over the chair which scolds me with a cloud of dust. My kitchen smells of cinnamon left steeping in yesterday’s tea. I switch off the lamp so that only refracted traffic light paints the walls in slow, involuntary frescoes.  

I undress and listen: somewhere above, the saxophone neighbor reaches a note so bittersweet it must be wearing shoes still wet from crossing a border. Somewhere below, a baby exercises single-minded lungs. I let my own inhaling conduct them both into a fragile orchestra.  

From the window I see the sliver of sky pinned between two taller buildings. A single star pulses like Morse: “I have stories about arriving late.” I blink dots and dashes back: “I am learning to be early to forgive.” Silence receives our correspondence and nods kindly.  

In bed, I open the Borges but don’t read; instead I place my palm on its spine and feel the weight of all circles missed: my mother’s arms, the ring I never asked back, planetary orbits that still mail light from centuries ago. Under this gravity I roll toward sleep, carrying the unrecognized home on my tongue like unswallowed sugar.  

Before dreams arrive, last synaptic fireflies spell the Akkadian noun once more: tuppu. Tablet, inscription, destiny. I imagine myself soft clay pressed with unforeseen footprints. Somewhere someone lifts the slab and leaves it in the sun to dry, my accidental biography becoming hard enough to survive millennia. When archaeologists decipher me, they will argue over whether that indentation near the left margin is a smile or a smudge.  

I hope they settle on smile, but perhaps memory prefers smudges; they are more truthful, white spaces where the wind of forgetting could enter. Either way, I will be a tablet abandoned by the tidal wave that translation makes inevitable. Meanwhile, the dark apartment thrums its own arrhythmia of pipes. radiator, heart, syntax. I stop translating and finally sleep.

Outside, dawn starts apprenticing at the color factory. Soon it will learn how to rinse midnight until only the thinnest bruise remains. Within hours commuters will lace up strategies for survival between pain au chocolat and podcasts roosting murder in their earbuds. The city will hum in tongues promiscuous and prophetic. And I, still new to every syllable, will step out again collecting borrowed letters like rainwater in my open palms—small shining declarations that I am almost here, that departure is only arrival turned politely inside out.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
